subcategory: Amazonian iconic edible nut canopy tree

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in full sun in deep, well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic tropical soil in zone 10–12; Brazil Nut is an enormous Amazonian canopy tree — one of the largest trees in the Amazon rainforest — bearing hard, cannonball-sized woody seed capsules (each containing 8–25 edible seeds) that require orchid pollinators and large mammals like agoutis for reproduction, and one of the most commercially important non-timber forest products in the Amazon.

directions: Plant Brazil Nut trees in deep, well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic soil in full sun with adequate space for their ultimate enormous size; they require mycorrhizal fungi associations established in forest soil for optimal growth; fruiting does not begin until trees are 10–15 years old and requires the presence of orchid bees (Eulaema spp.) for pollination of their complex flowers — making commercial fruiting difficult outside the Amazon Basin. Brazil Nut trees are among the most ecologically important trees in the Amazon — their hard, 4-inch woody seed pods (cocos) fall from the canopy with enough force to be fatal, making harvesting genuinely dangerous; they are collected by harvesters who enter the forest after the natural fall season. The seeds (nuts) are extraordinarily rich in selenium — a single Brazil nut provides the recommended daily allowance — and are one of the world's most nutritionally dense foods. Brazil Nut is also an indicator of primary forest health: the trees only reproduce successfully in intact, undisturbed Amazonian rainforest where their Euglossine bee pollinators (attracted exclusively by certain orchid species) and agouti seed dispersers are both present. This extraordinary ecological dependency makes Brazil Nuts one of the best arguments for conservation of intact Amazonian forest, as deforestation eliminates both the trees and their irreplaceable ecosystem partners.
sources: CIFOR (Center for International Forestry Research) — Brazil Nut research
Amazon Conservation Association — Bertholletia excelsa ecology

subcategory: Brazilian Cerrado iconic oilseed and fruit tree

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in full sun in well-drained to dry, deep, sandy to sandy-loam, acidic, lean cerrado-type soil in zone 10–12; Pequi is the defining fruit tree of the Brazilian Cerrado (tropical savanna) — a symbol of Minas Gerais and Goiás states — bearing unusual, golf-ball-to-softball-sized fruits with a hard outer husk enclosing a spiny, golden-yellow inner seed coat covered with intensely aromatic, oily, yellow-orange flesh with a distinctive, powerful, cheese-and-mango-and-toasted-nut flavor revered in central Brazilian cuisine.

directions: Plant Caryocar brasiliense in deep, well-drained, sandy, acidic, very lean cerrado soil in full sun; the tree is adapted to nutrient-poor savanna soils and performs poorly in rich, fertilized conditions; water moderately during establishment, then sparingly — it is drought-adapted. CAUTION: the inner seed coat (endocarp) is covered with sharp spines; eat the oily flesh from the outside of the seed coat with extreme care, using a scraping motion, or you will be impaled. Pequi is an ingredient of such cultural importance in Minas Gerais and the Cerrado of central Brazil that it has been called 'the soul of sertanejo cooking.' The intensely aromatic, golden-yellow oily flesh surrounding the spiny inner stone is eaten by scraping with the front teeth — a culturally specific technique that everyone from the region knows — in dishes like Galinhada com Pequi (chicken and rice with pequi), pamonha, and various cerrado preparations. The flavor is extraordinary and polarizing: powerful, complex, unmistakably distinctive, combining notes of ripe mango, roasted nuts, pungent tropical cheese, and a rich, almost buttery oiliness. The fruit oil is pressed for cosmetics, cooking, and traditional medicine. A crucial food for wildlife in the Cerrado: jaguars, tapirs, and many birds consume the fallen fruits. The tree is the symbol of Minas Gerais state and a cultural keystone of the cerrado region.
sources: Embrapa Cerrados — Caryocar brasiliense ecology and production
University of Brasília — pequi traditional uses

subcategory: Colombian tropical sapote Malvaceae fruit tree

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in full sun to partial shade in fertile, moist, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in zone 10–12; South American Sapote (Chupa-chupa, Sapote Costeño) is a fast-growing tropical fruit tree from the lowland forests of Colombia, Ecuador, Peru, and Brazil — bearing large, oval, salmon-orange fruits with very sweet, creamy, rich, apricot-orange flesh with a flavor combining apricot, peach, mango, and cream — among the finest-flavored of all Amazonian fruits and an important commercial fruit in Colombia.

directions: Plant Quararibea cordata in fertile, moist, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; water consistently throughout the year, maintaining soil moisture without waterlogging; the tree grows rapidly under good conditions and can begin fruiting in 3–5 years from a good-sized transplant. South American Sapote (chupa-chupa in Amazonian Colombia, where the name likely derives from the act of sucking the sweet flesh off the large seed) is one of the truly outstanding tropical fruits — combining the rich creaminess of mamey sapote with the bright tropical flavor of ripe apricot and mango in a way that is entirely its own. The orange-fleshed, salmon-skinned, football-sized fruits produced in large quantities on mature trees are eaten fresh out of hand or blended into juices, shakes, and ice creams. The tree is in the Malvaceae family (related to chocolate and kapok) and shares the fast growth and large size of its relatives. Fruiting is precocious for a tropical tree — beginning at 3–5 years from a good-quality transplant — and production can be heavy, with large trees bearing hundreds of fruits per season. Currently little-known outside Colombia and adjacent Amazonian countries but considered by tropical fruit enthusiasts as one of the best-tasting of all Neotropical fruits; wider cultivation in zone 10–12 tropical gardens is warranted.
sources: CATIE — Quararibea cordata tropical fruit research
Rare Fruit Council International — Quararibea cordata

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in full sun to partial shade in fertile, moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in zone 10–12; Olosapo (Zapotillo, Cacaito, Olosapo de Playa) is a medium-sized tropical fruit tree from the Pacific coast lowland forests of Guatemala, El Salvador, Honduras, Nicaragua, and Costa Rica — bearing oval to pear-shaped, russet-brown, thin-skinned fruits with rich, creamy, yellow flesh of exceptional flavor combining avocado, sweet potato, and almond — a prized traditional food of coastal Mesoamerica.

directions: Plant Couepia polyandra in fertile, moist, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; water regularly during establishment; trees begin fruiting at 5–8 years and can produce heavy crops of excellent-quality fruit once mature. Olosapo is a member of the Chrysobalanaceae family (relatives of coco plum) native to the Pacific coastal lowlands of Mesoamerica, where it is a traditional food tree in local communities in Guatemala, El Salvador, Honduras, and Costa Rica. The russet-brown to olive-green, oval fruits (resembling a small russet potato externally) contain very rich, creamy, yellow-orange flesh surrounding a single large seed; the texture is smooth and buttery, not unlike a ripe avocado, and the flavor is sweet and complex — combining notes of sweet potato, almond, and a mild tropical richness. The fruit is eaten fresh out of hand (removing the thin skin) or processed into beverages. The tree produces attractive, large, oval, glossy leaves and clusters of small white flowers followed by the distinctive fruits. Rarely seen outside its native range but considered by those who know it to be one of the finest-flavored Pacific coast Mesoamerican fruits; deserving wider cultivation in zone 10–12 tropical gardens in Florida, Hawaii, and southern California.
sources: CATIE — Couepia polyandra Central American fruit trees
Wilson, Perry E., 'Tropical Fruits of the World'
Rare Fruit Council International — Couepia polyandra

subcategory: West African edible black plum ornamental shade tree

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in full sun in well-drained, deep, moderately fertile, neutral to slightly alkaline, dry to moderately moist soil in zone 10–12; Black Plum (West African Plum, Loko, Donno) is a large, spreading shade tree native to the Sahel, savanna, and Sudan zones of West and Central Africa — highly valued across sub-Saharan Africa for its sweet, purple-black, date-sized plum-like fruits eaten fresh and for jam, its leaves consumed as a cooked vegetable, and its exceptionally dense, hard, golden-yellow wood — one of the most economically important multi-use agroforestry trees in West Africa.

directions: Plant Vitex doniana in well-drained, deep, slightly alkaline to neutral, lean to moderately fertile soil in full sun; highly drought-tolerant once established; prune young trees to develop good branching structure; the tree grows slowly at first then more vigorously once the root system is established. Black Plum is one of the most important multi-purpose trees of the African sahel and Sudan savanna zone — spread across a vast belt from Senegal and Gambia through Mali, Burkina Faso, Nigeria, and east to Sudan, Uganda, and Kenya. The sweet, date-like, purple-black fruits are eaten fresh throughout the region, made into local beverages, and dried for storage; the nutritional profile includes iron, calcium, and vitamins. Young leaves are cooked and eaten as a vegetable (especially important during the 'hungry season' before the harvest) and the leaves are also used medicinally for fever and rheumatism. The dense, golden-yellow heartwood is highly prized for furniture, carvings, drums, and mortars; a single tree can be worth significant sums in local markets. In the Sahel agroforestry system, Black Plum is a valued component of parkland farming systems (farmer-managed natural regeneration) where it provides food, shade for crops, fodder, and materials. An outstanding drought-tolerant shade tree for zone 10–12 dry tropical gardens.
sources: World Agroforestry Centre (ICRAF) — Vitex doniana agroforestry profile
FAO — Vitex doniana West African land use

subcategory: Central American Lecythidaceae tropical ornamental fruit

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in partial shade in fertile, moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in zone 10–12; Stinkwood Plum (Membrillo, Sachamango) is a magnificent ornamental small to medium tree from lowland rainforests of Colombia, Panama, Costa Rica, and Ecuador — producing enormous (up to 12 inches across), dramatically beautiful flower clusters of white to pink petals with hundreds of pink stamens, followed by large, ornamental, brown, pumpkin-shaped fruits with edible orange-yellow, mango-flavored flesh surrounding large seeds.

directions: Plant Gustavia superba in partial shade in fertile, moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil; maintain consistent moisture and protect from temperatures below 40°F; the tree is a good candidate for large containers in warm greenhouses in cooler climates. Gustavia superba is in the Lecythidaceae family — the same family as the Brazil nut and Cannonball Tree — and shares the family trait of producing extraordinarily ornamental, large, spectacular flowers; in Gustavia superba, the flowers can reach 10–12 inches across, with broad white to pink petals and a dramatic ring of hundreds of pink stamens forming a flower of breathtaking beauty. The common name 'Stinkwood' refers to the strong, pungent scent of the cut wood; the fruit name 'membrillo' (Spanish for quince) describes the pumpkin-shaped, brownish fruits. The orange-yellow, fibrous, mango-flavored flesh surrounding the large seeds is eaten locally in Colombia and Panama by both people and wildlife; it is not widely commercialized. The tree is exceptionally ornamental at every stage and stage-of-growth — its unusual, club-shaped leaves, spectacular flowers, and ornate fruits make it one of the most showstopping specimens for tropical garden collections. Rarely seen in cultivation outside botanical gardens and specialist collectors in zone 10–12.
